chunk_summary: Children with autism and neurodevelopmental disabilities often face severe feeding challenges, requiring tailored interventions, early referrals, and structured plans to address sensory avoidance, nutritional gaps, and caregiver burnout.
learning_objectives: Understand the feeding challenges specific to children with autism and neurodevelopmental disabilities, including sensory-based avoidance and rigid food preferences.
Learn strategies for structured feeding interventions, including measurable exposure targets and hunger rhythm protection.
Recognize the importance of early referral to feeding therapists and SLPs for improved outcomes in severe feeding difficulties.
Identify micronutrient deficiencies common in restricted diets and prescribe targeted supplementation.
key_facts: Children with ASD often meet Pediatric Feeding Disorder (PFD) criteria due to persistent food selectivity and nutritional dysfunction.
Screen for swallow-safety red flags such as coughing, choking, wet voice, and recurrent pneumonias, as aspiration risk is higher in this population.
Set measurable exposure targets, such as introducing one new texture or taste daily, to improve feeding skills.
Restricted diets may exclude iron-rich foods, animal-source foods, and dairy, increasing risk for deficiencies in iron, vitamin D, and vitamin B12.
Early referral to feeding therapists or SLPs is recommended when progress stalls, as sensory-based avoidance rarely resolves with brief advice.
reference_quote: Food selectivity and feeding problems in autism spectrum disorder often require structured interventions and early referral to specialists for improved outcomes. (Source: https://www.chla.org/blog/advice-experts/feeding-therapies-autism)
document_key_topics: Feeding difficulties in autism and neurodevelopmental disabilities
Sensory-based avoidance and rigid food preferences
Early referral to feeding therapists and SLPs
